claims.claim_text: The module presents a specific fintech etcd incident with quantified business scale, exact timing, same-rack quorum loss, API impact, and 847 frozen services.
claims.anchor_text: The SRE team at a fintech processing $2.1B in daily transactions got the alert at 3:12 AM: etcd leader election was failing. Three etcd members, all running on the same rack, had lost quorum when a switch firmware update went wrong. Without etcd, the Kubernetes API server became read-only — no new pods, no scaling, no deployments. The blast radius was total: 847 microservices frozen in place.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An etcd quorum failure can make the Kubernetes API effectively unavailable for writes, freezing normal cluster operations until quorum is restored.
claims.rationale: This is a precise operational anecdote with quantitative details but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a precise recovery timeline, stale snapshot window, lost state window, and quantified financial/SLA impact for the incident.
claims.anchor_text: Recovery took 4 hours and 23 minutes. Manual etcd restoration from a snapshot that was 6 hours stale. They lost 6 hours of ConfigMap updates, Secret rotations, and custom resource state. The post-incident review calculated $890K in direct losses and 3 SLA violations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Restoring etcd from an out-of-date snapshot can lose recent cluster-state changes and significantly extend an outage.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is valid, but the exact durations, losses, and SLA counts are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a later similar event caused zero downtime because the operator replaced a member and restored quorum in 47 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: Six months later, after deploying the etcd-operator with automated TLS, anti-affinity rules, and managed upgrades, a similar network event caused zero downtime — the operator detected the unhealthy member, replaced it, and restored quorum in 47 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Operator-managed recovery can reduce manual intervention during member failures, but any claimed outage outcome or timing needs a source.
claims.rationale: This is another specific incident claim with exact outcome and timing but no source.

claims.claim_text: etcd is Kubernetes' backing store for cluster state, and backups are necessary for recovery.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know first bullet / Part 1.1
claims.rationale: S001 directly states that etcd is Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data and emphasizes backups for recovery.

claims.claim_text: The official etcd operator release line is etcd-io/etcd-operator v0.2.0.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know second bullet / Part 2.1
claims.rationale: S002 is scoped to the official etcd-io operator release line and v0.2.0 features.

claims.claim_text: The old coreos/etcd-operator repository is archived.
claims.span_hint: Did You Know second bullet / Quiz 2 context
claims.rationale: S003 directly covers the archived status of the historical CoreOS operator repository.

claims.claim_text: The module says the old CoreOS operator has not been maintained since 2019.
claims.anchor_text: The old CoreOS operator hasn't been maintained since 2019.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The old CoreOS operator repository is archived.
claims.rationale: The archived status is supportable, but the precise maintenance cutoff year is not established by the shared pool.

claims.claim_text: etcd v3.6.0 introduced downgrade support, /livez and /readyz health endpoints, and more frequent compaction behavior.
claims.rationale: The etcd v3.6 announcement directly covers these version-specific features, but `etcd.io` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A single etcd write takes 3 network round trips, so performance is dominated by disk and network latency rather than CPU.
claims.anchor_text: **A single etcd write takes 3 network round trips** (leader propose → follower replicate → commit). This is why etcd performance is dominated by disk and network latency, not CPU. The operator's anti-affinity rules ensure members are on different nodes to survive hardware failures without sacrificing latency.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **etcd writes require coordination across the cluster**, so disk and network latency often matter more than CPU. Spreading members across nodes improves failure tolerance.
claims.rationale: The performance principle is real, but the exact round-trip count and the bundled anti-affinity conclusion are not cleanly backed by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes, the API server is the component that normally talks to etcd directly, and etcd access should be tightly restricted.
claims.rationale: S001 states that access to etcd is equivalent to root in the cluster and ideally only the API server should have access.

claims.claim_text: etcd-operator v0.2.0 adds built-in certificate management and supports Auto and Cert-Manager TLS providers.
claims.rationale: S002 is explicitly scoped to v0.2.0 certificate-management and TLS features.

claims.claim_text: TLS is only configured when explicitly enabled, and Auto is the default provider if TLS is enabled without a provider being specified.
claims.rationale: The operator announcement gives the exact defaulting behavior, but `etcd.io` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The operator's TLS feature covers both inter-member and client-to-member etcd communication.
claims.rationale: This is part of the v0.2.0 TLS feature set described by the shared operator release source.

claims.claim_text: The operator supports patch upgrades and one-minor-at-a-time upgrades, rejects skipped-minor upgrades, and validates upgrade paths for official etcd images.
claims.rationale: S002 is scoped to v0.2.0 upgrade support, including one-minor-at-a-time rules and validation behavior.

claims.claim_text: The upgrade workflow automatically rolls back on failure.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. Surfaces the failure so you can investigate before proceeding
claims.rationale: The available sources describe validation and supported paths, but not a documented automatic rollback guarantee.

claims.claim_text: The module's managed-Kubernetes warning names EKS, GKE, and AKS as offerings where the provider manages etcd for you.
claims.anchor_text: > **When NOT to use an operator**: If you're running managed Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS), the cloud provider manages etcd for you. The etcd-operator is for self-managed clusters — bare metal, kubeadm, or on-premises deployments where you own the control plane.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > **When NOT to use an operator**: Use the etcd-operator for clusters where you run etcd yourself. It is not relevant to hosted control planes where you do not manage the backing etcd.
claims.rationale: The general distinction is sound, but the named provider bundle needs provider-specific primary citations that this seed format cannot represent cleanly as one inline source.

claims.claim_text: The official etcd-operator release line includes gofail-based failure testing improvements.
claims.rationale: The v0.2.0 announcement explicitly mentions integrated gofail in e2e testing, but `etcd.io` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table asserts that aenix-io/etcd-operator's TLS management is specifically cert-manager-based.
claims.anchor_text: | TLS management | Auto + cert-manager | cert-manager | Manual |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | TLS management | Auto + cert-manager | Check current project docs | Manual |
claims.rationale: The aenix-specific matrix cell is uncited here and should not present a precise capability without a direct source.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table asserts that aenix-io/etcd-operator supports patch and minor managed upgrades.
claims.anchor_text: | Managed upgrades | Patch + minor | Patch + minor | Manual |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Managed upgrades | Patch + minor | Check current project docs | Manual |
claims.rationale: The exact aenix upgrade-support matrix needs its own primary source.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table asserts that aenix-io/etcd-operator has only limited failure testing.
claims.anchor_text: | Failure testing | gofail integration | Limited | None |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Failure testing | gofail integration | Check current project docs | Manual testing required |
claims.rationale: This comparative testing claim is too specific for the available sourced evidence.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table assigns maturity labels 'Pre-GA' and 'Proven' across the listed alternatives.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Maturity | Pre-GA (v0.2.0) | Check current release status | Manual operation patterns are well established |
claims.rationale: These labels are evaluative and need direct sourcing or softer phrasing.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says the archived CoreOS operator is unmaintained since 2019 and therefore a security risk.
claims.anchor_text: | Using the archived CoreOS etcd-operator | Unmaintained since 2019, security risks | Use etcd-io/etcd-operator (official) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Using the archived CoreOS etcd-operator | Archived and not the current project | Use etcd-io/etcd-operator (official) |
claims.rationale: The archived status is supportable, but the exact year and explicit security-risk conclusion are not sourced here.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table says operator v0.2.0 manages etcd v3.5.x and v3.6.x.
claims.anchor_text: | Confusing operator version with etcd version | Wrong version in spec | Operator v0.2.0 manages etcd v3.5.x/v3.6.x |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Confusing operator version with etcd version | Wrong version in spec | Check the operator release notes for supported etcd versions |
claims.rationale: The exact supported-version matrix should be sourced directly from release documentation instead of stated from memory.

further_reading.title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for etcd's role in Kubernetes, backups, member replacement, and production topology guidance.

further_reading.title: etcd-io/etcd-operator Releases
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical upstream release stream for the official operator and its supported feature set.

further_reading.title: etcd CHANGELOG-3.6
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful upstream release detail for version-specific etcd 3.6 behavior and upgrade-related changes.

notes: Quiz questions/answers, code blocks, and exercise steps were excluded per seed rules. The aenix comparison cells need a second pass with direct project sources if you want to keep a detailed side-by-side matrix instead of generic placeholders.
